subsystem: ImageMagick MATLAB (.mat) image coder — the ReadMATImage() decoder in coders/mat.c. It is a file-format parser that opens an untrusted MATLAB Level-5 / v4 array file, parses the 128-byte header and the sequence of length-prefixed 'miMATRIX' objects, and materializes each matrix as one or more Image frames (optionally zlib-decompressing object payloads).
provenance: Identified by the ImageMagick internal API vocabulary: MATHeader/MATLAB_HDR, ReadBlob/ReadBlobLSBLong/ReadBlobMSBLong, AcquireImage/DestroyImageList/OpenBlob, PixelPacket, QuantumInfo, GetAuthenticPixels/SyncAuthenticPixels/ImportQuantumPixels, ThrowReaderException, MagickCoreSignature, and the mx*_CLASS / mi* MATLAB type enums. The sample banner 'primevul_c_train_3301_vuln' and the file name '..._cwe416.c' mark this as the ReadMATImage reader, whose miMATRIX object loop is a well-known ImageMagick decoder.
trust_boundary: The entire blob read through OpenBlob/ReadBlob/ReadBlobXXXLong is attacker-controlled: a .mat file is externally supplied (uploaded image, thumbnailer input, delegate pipeline). Every field decoded from the wire is hostile — MATLAB_HDR.EndianIndicator, DataType, ObjectSize, StructureClass/StructureFlag, DimFlag, SizeX, SizeY, NameFlag, CellType, the per-object name size, the zlib-compressed payload, and the raster scanline bytes. None of these may be trusted for size, bound, or lifetime decisions without validation against the actual remaining blob length.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point of the coder: it takes a read-only ImageInfo (open parameters, ping flag, endianness) and an ExceptionInfo for error reporting, and returns a decoded Image list. The local state that governs the whole review is declared here: `image` is the primary output image, `image2` is a 'shadow' pointer that will alias either `image` itself or a freshly decompressed temporary image, and `rotated_image` receives the 90-degree rotated result. `MATLAB_HDR` is the parsed on-wire header struct whose fields drive every subsequent size decision. `ldblk` (ssize_t) is the per-scanline byte length computed from the attacker's SizeX and cell type. `BImgBuff` is the heap scanline buffer. `size`, `CellType`, `Unknown6` are size_t values decoded from the file. `filepos` is initialized to 0x80 (128), the fixed MATLAB header size, and is used as the object cursor.

Four function pointers select endian-specific readers at runtime. ReadBlobXXXLong/Short return 32/16-bit integers; ReadBlobDoublesXXX/FloatsXXX read `len` doubles/floats into a caller buffer. They are bound later from the file's EndianIndicator, so which byte order is used is itself attacker-selected. Crucially the Doubles/Floats readers take a `len` count parameter that must match the allocation of the destination buffer (BImgBuff).

Sanity asserts validate the caller-supplied handles, then the primary image is allocated with AcquireImage and the blob is opened for binary reading. On open failure the image list is destroyed and NULL returned. After this point `image` is a live object and `image2` is NULL; the invariant the rest of the function must maintain is precise ownership of `image` versus any shadow/temporary in `image2`.

The first 124 bytes are read into MATLAB_HDR.identific, a fixed descriptor buffer. If the leading 6 bytes are not 'MATLAB', the file is treated as the older MATLAB v4 format and delegated to ReadMATImageV4, then it jumps to END_OF_READING. Otherwise it is a v5 file and parsing continues. The read length (124) plus the later 2-byte version and 2-byte endian read totals the fixed 128-byte (0x80) header that filepos was seeded with.

The 2-byte EndianIndicator ('IM' = little-endian, 'MI' = big-endian) selects the reader function pointers and the image endianness. Any other value falls through to the MATLAB_KO label, which is the shared error exit: it destroys image2 (only if it is a distinct object) and clone_info and throws. MATLAB_KO is reached both structurally here and via many `goto MATLAB_KO` statements deeper in the parser, so its cleanup correctness is load-bearing for the whole function.

The main object loop begins at the byte after the header. Each object is a tag (DataType) plus a 32-bit ObjectSize plus a payload. Line 112 is the only length-vs-blob sanity check: it rejects the object if ObjectSize+filepos exceeds the real blob size. The cursor then advances by ObjectSize plus the two 4-byte tag/size words. ObjectSize is fully attacker-controlled and is the notional bound the later `ldblk*SizeY > ObjectSize` check relies on.

Per-object setup. clone_info is refreshed each iteration. Line 121 aliases `image2 = image`: for a normal (uncompressed) object, image2 and image are the SAME object. If the object is miCOMPRESSED, decompress_block returns a brand-new temporary image and image2 is repointed to it, then the true DataType is re-read from the decompressed stream. From here on the code reads structure fields from `image2`, which is either the primary image or a distinct temporary — the two-headed ownership that the lifetime bugs stem from.

Non-matrix objects are skipped (freeing a distinct image2). For a matrix, several 'unknown' header words are consumed, and unknown5 is split into StructureClass (low byte, the numeric cell class) and StructureFlag (next byte, carrying FLAG_COMPLEX/FLAG_LOGICAL bits). DimFlag selects the matrix dimensionality, and SizeX/SizeY are the pixel dimensions — both are raw 32-bit little/big-endian values straight from the file and are used unbounded to size the raster buffer and loop counts.

DimFlag decodes dimensionality: 8 = 2D grayscale (z=1), 12 = 3D RGB requiring exactly z=3 planes, 16 = 4D animation with z in {1,3} and a Frames count. This constrains z to {1,3}, which later bounds the z2qtype[] index and the plane loop. Frames==0 is rejected, and Frames is rate-limited via AcquireMagickResource(ListLengthResource,...). Note that on the 12/16 paths an extra ReadBlobXXXLong is consumed for the plane count and Unknown6, shifting the stream position for subsequent fixed reads.

Flag1 and NameFlag shorts are read, then StructureClass is validated against the whitelist of supported mx*_CLASS values. If unsupported, image2 is closed and deleted and the function throws. Note this branch calls DeleteImageFromList(&image2) even when image2 == image (the uncompressed case), which frees the primary image while the later shared error/return cleanup may still reference it — a lifetime hazard flagged below.

The object name is skipped: NameFlag 0 reads a 4-byte length, rounds it up to a 4-byte boundary, and seeks past it; NameFlags 1-4 read a 4-byte inline name into `size`; anything else jumps to MATLAB_KO. CellType (the numeric element type, mi*) is then read and selects the sample width. A trailing 4-byte 'data size' is read into `size`. Note both `ReadBlob(..,4,..&size)` writes only 4 bytes into the 8-byte size_t `size` on LP64, leaving the upper 4 bytes indeterminate.

CellType maps the element type to image depth and, most importantly, to `ldblk`, the byte length of one scanline: SizeX for 8-bit, 2*SizeX, 4*SizeX, or 8*SizeX for wider types. SizeX is an attacker-controlled 32-bit value and the multiplier reaches 8, so this product is the central integer that both sizes the heap buffer and drives the per-row ReadBlob length. There is no upper bound applied to SizeX before this multiply.

Image geometry is set from SizeX/SizeY, zero dimensions are rejected, and line 331 is the sole guard tying the raster size back to the object size: it rejects when ldblk*SizeY exceeds ObjectSize. This is the safety check that is supposed to prevent reading more scanline data than the object holds, but it operates on ldblk and SizeY that were computed by an unchecked multiply, and ObjectSize is itself attacker-chosen, so the guard is only as strong as those inputs not having wrapped.

In ping mode (dimensions only) the code swaps columns/rows and jumps to done_reading. Otherwise it calls SetImageExtent to allocate the pixel cache for columns*rows, then acquires a QuantumInfo describing the pixel import format. The `return(DestroyImageList(image))` on SetImageExtent failure does not free a distinct image2, an ownership asymmetry relative to the other error exits.

The scanline buffer is allocated as ldblk * sizeof(double) bytes via AcquireQuantumMemory (which internally checks the two-factor multiply for overflow) and zeroed. For float/double cells, CalcMinMax pre-scans the object to find min/max. The allocation deliberately over-allocates by sizeof(double) per unit so wider reads fit — but this only holds if ldblk itself was computed without wrapping.

The raster loop reads bottom-up: for each of SizeY rows it acquires a writable pixel row, reads exactly ldblk bytes from image2 into BImgBuff, applies logical/sign fixups, and imports the bytes into the pixel cache with ImportQuantumPixels using z2qtype[z] as the channel selector. The outer do/while iterates the color planes via z (constrained to {0,1,3} by the DimFlag logic). z2qtype[z] is indexed by z; FixSignedValues writes MATLAB_HDR.SizeX samples into the row `q`. Correctness depends on ldblk matching both the buffer size and the pixel-cache row width.

If FLAG_COMPLEX is set, a second element type and its size are re-read from image2, and the imaginary part is loaded. Here ReadBlobDoublesXXX/FloatsXXX are called with `ldblk` as the element COUNT, i.e. they read ldblk doubles = ldblk*8 bytes (or ldblk floats = ldblk*4). Because BImgBuff is ldblk*sizeof(double) = ldblk*8 bytes, the double path exactly fills it, but note CellType is re-read here from the file, so a complex object can specify a wider element than the one that sized ldblk/BImgBuff.

After the raster is loaded the image type is finalized, and line 480 clears image2 when it aliases image ('remove shadow copy before rotation'). The image is rotated 90 degrees; on success the rotated image inherits the blob reference and is spliced into the list via AppendImageToList, then the original `image` is removed with DeleteImageFromList(&image). DeleteImageFromList frees the object `image` pointed to and repoints `image` to a neighbor (the rotated image). Any other pointer still aliasing the freed original is now dangling — this is the crux of the lifetime problem.

The done_reading epilogue frees a distinct temporary image2 and its clone_info temp file, then, unless at EOF, allocates the next frame image and resets its geometry. The scanline buffer and quantum_info are released. If more frames remain (Frames was decremented), z is restored and image2 is re-aliased to the (new) image and control jumps back to NEXT_FRAME to read the next plane/frame without re-parsing the object header. This is where image2 gets re-tied to whichever object `image` currently is.

End of the object loop body: a distinct shadow image2 (a decompressed temporary) is deleted and its backing temp file unlinked, and clone_info is destroyed. Then the while loop repeats for the next object. Note the same DeleteImageFromList(&image2) pattern appears at done_reading and here, so on some control paths image2 can be operated on twice, and its relationship to `image` after rotation is not re-validated.

Function epilogue: after closing the blob it rewinds the list removing empty (0x0) frames, and while doing so nulls image2 if the frame being deleted happens to equal image2 (an attempt to avoid a stale image2). clone_info is finally destroyed. Line 605 destroys image2 only if it differs from image. Line 608's ThrowReaderException lacks a trailing semicolon before `return`, indicating this is the raw (pre-fix) form. The final image list is returned. The whole epilogue must reconcile every path where `image` or `image2` may have been freed earlier — the guard `tmp == image2` at line 577 only catches image2 aliases that are still list members, not ones already freed by DeleteImageFromList(&image) during rotation.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Use-after-free / stale-alias (CWE-416), the headline defect. `image2 = image` is set at line 121 (and re-set at line 532 in the multi-frame path). Line 480 only nulls image2 when it CURRENTLY equals image; but in the multi-frame animation case image2 is re-aliased to `image` at line 532 AFTER this guard already ran, and other error branches (e.g. line 229 DeleteImageFromList(&image2) with image2==image) free the primary object while another alias still points at it. When DeleteImageFromList(&image) at line 494 frees the original image object during rotation, any surviving image2 alias to that same object becomes dangling; the terminal cleanup `image2=DestroyImage(image2)` at line 605 (or the `tmp == image2` comparison at line 577) then reads/frees freed memory. An attacker crafts a multi-frame or compressed .mat so that image2 still aliases a freed image at function exit, yielding a use-after-free/double-free and potential remote code execution. Invariant: every path that frees the object `image` points to must first null out every alias (image2) to that same object, and no alias may be dereferenced or freed after the object is deleted.

Premature free of the primary image via the shadow alias (CWE-416). On the unsupported-StructureClass path, at this point image2 was set to `image` at line 121 (uncompressed object). CloseBlob(image2) closes the primary image's blob and DeleteImageFromList(&image2) frees the primary image object, but `image` still points to the freed memory. ThrowReaderException performs a longjmp/return to the shared cleanup that dereferences `image` (list rewind at 570-584, `image != image2` test at 605), operating on freed memory. An attacker supplies a matrix with a valid DimFlag but an out-of-whitelist StructureClass to reach this branch. Invariant: image2 must not be deleted when it aliases image, or `image` must be cleared in lockstep; the correct fix deletes image2 only when image2 != image.

Integer overflow in the scanline-length computation (CWE-190) feeding an under-sized allocation and subsequent out-of-bounds access (CWE-787/125). SizeX is a fully attacker-controlled 32-bit value; the multipliers 2/4/8 are applied in `int`/`unsigned` width, so e.g. SizeX = 0x20000001 with the 8x path wraps to a small ldblk. That small ldblk sizes BImgBuff at line 377 (AcquireQuantumMemory(ldblk,sizeof(double))) and the memset at line 386, while image->columns is still the true large SizeX (line 326). ImportQuantumPixels at 418/428 then imports based on image->columns and FixSignedValues(q,MATLAB_HDR.SizeX) at 433 writes SizeX samples, reading past the short BImgBuff / writing past the row. The line 331 guard `(unsigned long)ldblk*MATLAB_HDR.SizeY > MATLAB_HDR.ObjectSize` does not catch this because it uses the already-wrapped ldblk. Invariant: SizeX must be range-checked (e.g. against ObjectSize and a sane maximum) BEFORE the multiply, and the multiply must be done in a width that cannot wrap.

Weak / overflowable bounds check (CWE-190). This is the only guard that ties raster size to object size, but (a) both ldblk and SizeY are attacker-controlled and their product `(unsigned long)ldblk*SizeY` can itself overflow (on ILP32 unsigned long is 32-bit; even on LP64 ldblk may already have wrapped), and (b) MATLAB_HDR.ObjectSize is attacker-chosen and was only loosely validated at line 112 against the blob size. An attacker can pick ObjectSize large enough to satisfy this comparison while still triggering the short-buffer condition above, defeating the guard. Invariant: the check must be computed in a non-wrapping width and must bound against the ACTUAL remaining bytes of the object payload, not an attacker-declared ObjectSize.

Type-confused buffer reuse in the complex-value path (CWE-125/787). CellType is RE-READ from the file here, independently of the CellType that originally sized ldblk and BImgBuff at lines 261-316. If the real (first) cell was a narrow type (e.g. miUINT8, so ldblk = SizeX bytes and BImgBuff = SizeX*8 bytes) but the complex part declares miDOUBLE, ReadBlobDoublesXXX(image2, ldblk, BImgBuff) reads ldblk doubles = ldblk*8 bytes into BImgBuff. For miUINT8 BImgBuff is exactly ldblk*8 so it fits, but the arithmetic depends entirely on ldblk not having wrapped and on the buffer's sizeof(double) over-allocation; any path where ldblk was derived from a wider first type while the complex reader multiplies again is an out-of-bounds read/write. Invariant: the complex-part element type and length must be re-validated against the existing BImgBuff allocation before reading.

Unbounded name-length seek with wrap potential (CWE-191/CWE-20). `size` is read from the file, incremented by 4 and rounded up, then used as a relative SeekBlob offset. `size + 3 + 1` on a size near SIZE_MAX/near UINT32_MAX can wrap, and the resulting seek is unchecked against the object/blob bounds, letting an attacker reposition the read cursor arbitrarily (forwards) so that subsequent fixed reads consume unintended bytes. The impact is limited (SeekBlob within a file is not itself a memory-safety bug) but it corrupts the parser's notion of object boundaries. Invariant: name size must be bounded against the remaining object bytes before seeking, computed without overflow.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
(void) ReadBlob(image2, 4, (unsigned char *) &size); /* Object name string */
...
    (void) ReadBlob(image2, 4, (unsigned char *) &size);     /* data size */
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Partial write into a wider variable leaving uninitialized bytes (CWE-457), plus ignored short-read (CWE-252). On LP64 `size` is an 8-byte size_t but only 4 bytes are written, so its upper 32 bits retain whatever the previous use of `size` left (or garbage), and the ReadBlob return value is discarded so a truncated file that returns fewer than 4 bytes still proceeds. Here `size` from these two reads is not subsequently used for a memory operation in the shown code, so the direct impact is low, but relying on a half-initialized size_t is a latent hazard if the implementation later trusts it. Invariant: read the exact width of the destination (or zero it first) and check the ReadBlob return count.

Object-size cursor arithmetic overflow (CWE-190). MATLAB_HDR.ObjectSize is attacker-controlled and is added to filepos both in the guard and in the cursor advance `filepos += ObjectSize + 4 + 4`. If ObjectSize is near the type maximum, `ObjectSize+filepos` can wrap and pass the `> GetBlobSize` check while the object actually extends past the blob, or filepos can be advanced non-monotonically, driving the loop to re-parse or read out of the intended region. The guard uses ObjectSize before it is bounded to a sane maximum. Invariant: validate ObjectSize against the remaining blob length using a non-wrapping comparison (e.g. ObjectSize > GetBlobSize - filepos) before trusting it.

summary: Attacker-controlled MATLAB .mat parser (ImageMagick ReadMATImage). The dominant risk is a use-after-free / double-free from tangled image / image2 ownership: image2 aliases the primary image, several error and rotation paths free one while the other still points to it, and the terminal cleanup then dereferences/destroys the freed object (CWE-416). Secondary risks are integer-overflow in the ldblk scanline-length computation from unbounded SizeX feeding an under-sized heap buffer (CWE-190/787) and a type-confused complex-part read. Multiple missing bounds checks compound remote exploitability from a single crafted image.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Its attacker-controlled fields (ObjectSize, SizeX, SizeY, DataType, StructureClass/Flag, DimFlag, CellType, NameFlag) drive every size and lifetime decision.
Fixed 124-byte read into a fixed field with an exact length check; only strncmp(...,6) is applied — no length/buffer mismatch.
Exactly 2 bytes read into a 2-byte field with length-checked ReadBlob; only used for strncmp of 2 bytes to select readers.
Read but not used for any size/lifetime decision.
Compared against enum tags only (miCOMPRESSED/miMATRIX); no arithmetic. Its re-read after decompress is covered under the decompress re-parse note.
Attacker-controlled length used in cursor arithmetic (line 112,114) and as the raster bound (line 331); overflow/weak-guard hazard.
Unbounded 32-bit value multiplied by up to 8 to compute ldblk; central integer-overflow source.
Attacker-controlled row count; loop bound and factor in the overflowable ldblk*SizeY guard.

StructureClass is whitelisted (214-224); StructureFlag bits gate FLAG_LOGICAL/FLAG_COMPLEX branches. The dangerous side-effect (freeing image2==image on the unsupported branch) is captured as a finding on lines 226-233, not here.
Switched over a closed set with default-reject; constrains z to {1,3}. No direct memory contract of its own.
Case 0 computes an unbounded rounded name size then SeekBlob; default rejects. Name-seek hazard flagged.
Consumed to advance the stream position; not used in size or pointer arithmetic (Unknown6 explicitly discarded).
Selects sample width/ldblk; re-read in the complex path (450) enabling type confusion against the existing buffer.
4-byte reads into 8-byte size_t (uninitialized upper bytes) and unbounded rounded name-length seek.
Scanline byte length from overflowable multiply; sizes BImgBuff and drives every raster read.
Fixed heap buffer sized by ldblk; under-allocation on ldblk wrap yields OOB read/write. Covered by the ldblk overflow finding.
The two-factor API checks its own multiply for overflow; the defect is the caller passing an already-wrapped ldblk (covered by the ldblk finding).
Rejected when 0 and rate-limited via AcquireMagickResource(ListLengthResource,Frames); bounded before use.
DimFlag logic constrains z to {1,3} (then z=0), bounding the z2qtype[z] index and plane loop.
Index z is bounded to a small set by the DimFlag validation; no attacker-controlled out-of-range index reaches it.

Bound to fixed internal functions selected by the 'IM'/'MI' indicator with a default-reject; not attacker-writable pointers. The Doubles/Floats len-vs-buffer risk is covered by the complex-path finding.
The core use-after-free/double-free contract: shadow aliasing plus DeleteImageFromList of one alias while the other survives.
Refreshed/destroyed each iteration with NULL guards; its temp-file cleanup is bound to the image2 lifetime already flagged.
Object cursor combined with attacker ObjectSize in overflowable additions (line 112,114).
Implementation not shown; its output pointer feeds the image2 lifetime finding and the re-read DataType is a stream-position effect, not a distinct memory contract in the shown code.
Assigned then explicitly discarded with (void) sample_size at line 325; no effect.
Macro-form artifact (macro supplies control flow); not a security contract, though it marks this as the pre-fix variant.

findings.title: Use-after-free / double-free from image2 shadow-alias outliving freed image
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0218_cwe416.c (ReadMATImage) and RotateImage/DeleteImageFromList in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: Every code path that frees the object `image` points to must first NULL out every other pointer (image2) that aliases the same object; no alias may be dereferenced or freed after the object is deleted, and image2 must be nulled BEFORE, not only when it currently equals image at one specific point.
findings.failure_mode: image2 is aliased to image at line 121 and re-aliased at line 532 on the multi-frame path. The line 480 `if(image2==image) image2 = NULL` guard runs once, but the animation loop re-establishes image2==image afterward, and rotation frees the original image via DeleteImageFromList(&image) at line 494. The surviving image2 alias is now dangling; the epilogue at line 605 `image2=DestroyImage(image2)` (or the `tmp == image2` handling at 577) then frees/reads the already-freed object. A crafted multi-frame or miCOMPRESSED .mat triggers the dangling alias, giving use-after-free/double-free and likely remote code execution.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether, on the multi-frame (DimFlag 16) and miCOMPRESSED paths, image2 can still point at the object freed by DeleteImageFromList(&image) at line 494 when execution reaches line 577/605; the fixed code sets image2=NULL immediately after any free of image and only deletes image2 when image2 != image.

findings.title: Primary image freed through image2 alias on unsupported-StructureClass error path
findings.bug_class: Use-after-free (free of aliased primary image before shared cleanup dereferences it)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0218_cwe416.c (ReadMATImage) and DeleteImageFromList in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: image2 must only be closed/deleted when it is a distinct temporary (image2 != image); when image2 aliases image, freeing it invalidates `image`, which the ThrowReaderException cleanup path still uses.
findings.failure_mode: For an uncompressed object image2 == image (line 121). Reaching the unsupported-StructureClass branch calls CloseBlob(image2) and DeleteImageFromList(&image2), freeing the object `image` also points to. ThrowReaderException transfers to the shared epilogue (list rewind at 570-584 and `image != image2` test at 605) which dereferences the freed `image`. An attacker sets a valid DimFlag with a StructureClass outside the whitelist to reach this branch.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify that this branch is reachable with image2==image and that the post-throw cleanup dereferences image; confirm the fix guards the delete with image2 != image (or nulls image).

findings.title: Integer overflow computing ldblk from attacker SizeX under-allocates scanline buffer
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow leading to heap under-allocation and out-of-bounds access
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0218_cwe416.c (ReadMATImage); ImportQuantumPixels/FixSignedValues in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: SizeX must be validated against a sane maximum and against the object payload length BEFORE multiplying by the per-element width, and the multiply must use a width (size_t) that cannot wrap; image->columns (=SizeX) must match the width used to size BImgBuff.
findings.failure_mode: SizeX is an unchecked 32-bit file value. `2/4/8 * SizeX` is evaluated in 32-bit and wraps for large SizeX (e.g. 8 * 0x20000001), yielding a small ldblk that under-sizes BImgBuff at line 377 while image->columns keeps the true large SizeX (line 326). ImportQuantumPixels (418/428) and FixSignedValues(q,SizeX) (433) then process image->columns samples, reading past BImgBuff and/or writing past the pixel row. The line 331 ldblk*SizeY guard uses the already-wrapped ldblk and does not catch it.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether the multiply is performed in 32-bit before the (ssize_t) cast and whether any bound on SizeX exists prior to line 270; confirm ImportQuantumPixels reads image->columns rather than ldblk-derived counts.

findings.title: Raster bound check is itself overflowable and trusts attacker ObjectSize
findings.invariant: The raster size must be bounded against the ACTUAL remaining object bytes using a non-wrapping computation; the comparison must not rely on an attacker-declared ObjectSize nor on a product that can overflow unsigned long.
findings.failure_mode: `(unsigned long)ldblk*SizeY` can overflow (unsigned long is 32-bit on ILP32; ldblk may already be wrapped), and ObjectSize is attacker-chosen (only loosely validated at line 112). An attacker picks ObjectSize large enough to satisfy the comparison while the true payload is short, so the guard passes and the per-row ReadBlob at line 409 over-reads / the buffer math is defeated.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the multiply width and whether ObjectSize was bounded to the real blob remainder before this check; a correct guard compares against GetBlobSize-filepos with overflow-safe arithmetic.

findings.title: Complex-part element type re-read enables type confusion against fixed BImgBuff
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0218_cwe416.c (ReadMATImage); ReadBlobDoublesXXX/CalcMinMax in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: The complex-part CellType and its declared length must be re-validated against the existing BImgBuff allocation (which was sized from the real part's CellType/ldblk) before any read; the two element types must be consistent.
findings.failure_mode: The imaginary-part CellType is re-read from the file independently of the real-part CellType that sized ldblk/BImgBuff. ReadBlobDoublesXXX(image2, ldblk, BImgBuff) uses ldblk as an element count of doubles (ldblk*8 bytes). If the real part sized ldblk for a wider type or ldblk wrapped, the double/float reads and InsertComplex*Row can exceed BImgBuff. An attacker sets FLAG_COMPLEX and mismatched real/imag element types.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify the byte counts ReadBlobDoublesXXX/ReadBlobFloatsXXX read for a given ldblk against the BImgBuff size, and whether the re-read CellType is validated against the original.

findings.title: Object cursor arithmetic overflow with attacker-controlled ObjectSize
findings.invariant: ObjectSize must be validated as ObjectSize <= GetBlobSize - filepos using non-wrapping arithmetic before it is added to filepos or trusted as a payload length.
findings.failure_mode: ObjectSize is an attacker 32-bit value added to filepos in both the guard and the cursor advance. A near-maximum ObjectSize can wrap `ObjectSize+filepos` so the `> GetBlobSize` check passes although the object exceeds the blob, or advances filepos non-monotonically, causing the loop to re-parse or read outside the intended object region.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the width of MagickSizeType/MagickOffsetType and whether the addition can wrap for the maximum ObjectSize; a safe form subtracts filepos from GetBlobSize instead of adding.

findings.title: Unbounded, overflow-prone object-name seek
findings.bug_class: Unchecked length / integer wrap in relative seek
findings.invariant: The name length must be bounded against the remaining object bytes and rounded without overflow before being used as a SeekBlob offset.
findings.failure_mode: `size` is read from the file and `size + 3 + 1` can wrap near the type maximum; the result is used as an unchecked relative seek, letting an attacker desynchronize the parser's object boundaries so subsequent fixed reads consume unintended bytes. Impact is limited to parser confusion rather than direct memory corruption in the shown code.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether name size is bounded against the object length and whether the rounding can overflow before SeekBlob.

findings.title: Four-byte read into eight-byte size_t leaves uninitialized upper bytes and ignores short reads
findings.bug_class: Use of partially-uninitialized variable / unchecked read length
findings.invariant: Read the exact width of the destination scalar (or zero-initialize it first) and check the ReadBlob return count so a truncated file cannot leave the value partly indeterminate.
findings.failure_mode: On LP64 `size` is 8 bytes but only 4 are written, so the upper 32 bits retain prior/garbage contents; the ReadBlob return value is discarded so a short file still proceeds. In the shown code `size` from these reads is not used for a memory operation, keeping impact low, but any implementation that later trusts `size` inherits a latent integer/length hazard.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no downstream use of `size` from lines 247/258 performs allocation or bounds math on the half-initialized value.
